title: Sorting Data in ProcScript
text: Sorting Data in ProcScript Uniface provides ProcScript instructions for sorting entity occurrences in hitlists and lists created in ProcScript. It is possible to perform multi-level sorting, enabling you to sort by several fields, each with its own sort criteria. You can use the following ProcScript instructions for sorting lists such as property lists, ValRep lists, and nested lists of various kinds. Table 1. ProcScript Instructions for Sorting Data ProcScript Description $nlssortorder Return or set the current sort ordering rules. sort Sort entities on one or more fields. sort/list Sort an associative list, an indexed list, or an indexed list of sublists. WHY the distinctions between sort/list and $sortlist? sort/list Sort an associative list or an indexed list. $sortlistid Sort an associative list, an indexed list, or an associative list of sublists. Sort Element and Sort Options When using the ProcScript sorting instructions, you need to specify the data to sort (an entity or a list), and optionally, one or more elements to sort by. For each element, you can specify the sort options. Although the exact syntax of ProcScript statements and functions is different, the syntax for specifying the sort options is the same: { ElementToSortBy : SortOrder Unique Type { ; ElementToSortBy : SortOrder Unique Type } } The ElementToSortBy and sort options are specified as a comma-separated or GOLD ; ( ; ) separated list. All sort options are optional. After the ElementToSortBy , they can be defined in any order. ElementToSortBy —entity field ( sort and sort/e only), list item, or sublist item on which to sort. Table 2. Sort Options Sort Option Valid Values Meaning Order ascending | A descending | D Sort order Unique unique | U Keep only unique items; discard duplicates. Example: Removing Items with Duplicate Values Type Determines how sorting is performed by specifying the locale, case sensitivity, level, or data type. If not specified, the default is nlslocale . nlslocale | NLS Sort as a string using the value of $nlssortorder . If this is not set, the value of $nlslocale is used. If this is not set, a binary sort is performed. Sorting Based on Locale CaseSensitive | CS | classic Sort as a case-sensitive string, ignoring locale-based sorting conventions. CaseInsensitive | CI Sort as a case-insensitive string, using the 1-to-1 character matching for Unicode uppercase and lowercase rules. level Sort data as level ID ( n.n.n ), so that 9.2 is sorted before 10.1 . Example: Sorting by Hierarchical Level Note: When specifying the following sort options, it is assumed the data is in a format that can be interpreted as the specified data type. Example: Sorting Using Data Type . Numeric Sort data as Numeric Float Sort data as Float Boolean Sort data as Boolean Date Sort data as Date Time Sort data as Time Note: When specifying the following sort options, Uniface converts the data to the specified data type before sorting: $number Sort Data as $number ( String ) $float Sort Data as $float ( String ) $boolean Sort Data as $boolean ( String ) $date Sort Data as $date ( String ) $time Sort Data as $clock ( String ) $datim Sort Data as $datim ( String ) $string Sort Data as $string ( String ) For examples demonstrating how you can use the sort options to control sorting behavior, see Using Sort Options . For examples of list sorting, see Sorting Lists and Sublists . Related Links Lists and Sublists A list is a group of related items homogeneous items collected into a single unit, the list. Every item in a list is assumed to have the same structure. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
